blocks.content: The law was lifted and the tourists regained their human forms, they were horrified to see their naked bodies.
Chaos ensued. Women screamed and covered themselves, while men fumbled around looking for places to hide.
Shirone and his group managed to evacuate everyone to an inn with the help of people outside the city walls.
Sitting on the railing of a café terrace, Shirone watched the tourists filling up the buildings.
Some were threatening to sue the tourism board, while others were loudly demanding to see the manager.
They too had encountered a being from heaven. The implications of today's events were unpredictable.
Shirone looked back at Kangnan. She too was watching the tourists, but her mind seemed elsewhere.

blocks.speaker: Shirone
blocks.content: What do we do now? Should we try to convince the people?

blocks.speaker: Kangnan
blocks.content: Is that necessary?

blocks.speaker: Shirone
blocks.content: Pardon?

blocks.speaker: Kangnan
blocks.content: We don't need to bother with trivial matters. The association will send people. They'll handle it.

blocks.speaker: Shirone
blocks.content: But what if it gets harder to manage over time? What if rumors spread?

blocks.speaker: Kangnan
blocks.content: Just announce it as if the myth of the Ice Queen was true. Whether they believe it or not, it contaminates the information. But it doesn't matter if we tell the truth because it's not something ordinary people can handle anyway.
Kangnan had often encountered such situations. Despite the potential for chaos if information about heaven spread worldwide, she was certain nothing significant would happen.
People would still worry about their daily meals, lose sleep over important contracts the next day, and fret over how to raise their growing children.
Kangnan looked back at the group following her. Despite having no specific orders, they hadn't left her side.
They knew it was a situation beyond their control to manage.
But to Kangnan, it was merely an annoyance.

blocks.speaker: Kangnan
blocks.content: The association will decide your fate too. So stop following me. Run away if you want.
The group hung their heads dejectedly.

They understood what it meant to be adversaries of the Magic Association. Even if they went to prison, as long as they were part of the Red Line, their magician's licenses wouldn't be revoked. But if they ran, the Black Line was their only refuge—a world inhabited by those lost to madness.
The group didn't have what it takes to survive there.

blocks.speaker: Shirone
blocks.content: Yes, we're sorry. We'll turn ourselves in when the association people arrive.
Amy silently watched the departing group.
This was as far as they could share their lives.
Whatever happened, they had to continue living, fighting fiercely to be the protagonists of their own lives.
After ensuring follow-up measures were in place, Kangnan prepared to leave.
Before speaking, she gave Shirone a hard look.
He looked much younger once the Diamond Armor was deactivated.
Of course, she wasn't foolish enough to judge someone by their appearance.
However, she considered that such features could be problematic within the association.
Gaold's proposal was indeed radical.

blocks.speaker: Kangnan
blocks.content: Shirone. It's time to leave. I'll wait for you in the carriage. Hurry up and come along.

blocks.speaker: Shirone
blocks.content: Yes, I'll be right there.
Shirone was ready to follow Kangnan to the Magic Association.
The chance to meet Gaold was rare, but he was also curious about the situation with Miro.

blocks.speaker: Shirone
blocks.content: I might meet Rian and Tess in the capital.
Shirone grabbed his backpack and approached Amy.

blocks.speaker: Shirone
blocks.content: I'm sorry it turned out this way.
He felt ashamed facing Amy. He had wanted to enjoy a proper vacation this time, but once again, he had put her in danger.

blocks.speaker: Amy
blocks.content: I'll tell you plainly, I won't travel with you again.

blocks.speaker: Shirone
blocks.content: Ha ha! I'm really sorry. I don't know what to say.
Amy smiled and shook her head.

blocks.speaker: Amy
blocks.content: Thanks, Shirone. Because of you, I've gained confidence. I think I can work hard again now.

blocks.speaker: Shirone
blocks.content: He felt it too. Not only had he overpowered the group, but the extreme heat of the Inferno was a spell difficult for others their age to execute without Amy.
He hadn't asked about her enlightenment during the fight.

blocks.speaker: Shirone
blocks.content: There are as many enlightenments as there are people, and Amy's style was unique.
Perhaps they would discuss it someday.

blocks.speaker: Shirone
blocks.content: I'll contact you when I get back. And Dante, thanks for looking after Amy.
Dante shrugged.
Unexpectedly tasked with escorting a rival's girlfriend, it wasn't a bad deal.
Both the talks with heaven and Miro seemed to suggest that Shirone and Amy knew something.
He planned to ask about heaven on the way.

blocks.speaker: Dante
blocks.content: Don't know what this is all about, but go well.

blocks.speaker: Shirone
blocks.content: Yeah. See you at school.
As Shirone left, Dante and Amy also turned to leave.
Near the exit, Dante pointed to a café.
How about a coffee before we go? You're buying, of course.

blocks.speaker: Amy
blocks.content: Trying to cling to the very end! Buy your own! You've got plenty of money!
Ah. I'm escorting you home, a coffee wouldn't hurt… huh?
Dante turned around at a noise.
Liria was standing there as she had when they first met, a large backpack on her back.
With the Lawmaker sealed, she should return to the Akeanis order to report.
Likely, after today, she wouldn't meet Dante again. Magicians and sorcerers, beings of light and shadow, naturally walked different paths.
But Liria was left with regrets.
If she hadn't met Dante, this mission would likely have ended in failure.
It was hard to imagine a wandering sorcerer sharing affection with someone, but because she had relied on him considerably, she couldn't simply cut off her feelings.

blocks.speaker: Liria
blocks.content: I'll buy the coffee.
Hmm.
Dante read the affection in Liria's eyes.
Truly, she was a woman who couldn't hide her feelings.
He wasn't one to enjoy flings, but he wasn't in the habit of turning away those who came to him.
He pulled her close and pointed somewhere.
Okay, forget coffee. How about we find somewhere quiet to go in?
Liria's face flushed as she looked where Dante pointed.
A big sign for an inn hung prominently.

blocks.speaker: Liria
blocks.content: Are you... what are you... saying...?
Ha ha! No need to back down. We're all adults here. Let's not waste time.
Liria groaned.
She could tolerate his self-centered personality to an extent, but his promiscuity was unacceptable.

blocks.speaker: Liria
blocks.content: Such a flirt! Let go!
When Liria pushed him away, Dante easily stepped back.

blocks.speaker: Dante
blocks.content: A flirt? How is it flirting when we haven't done anything yet?
Forget it! I must have lost my mind! I'm going back to the order!
Dante chuckled.
If this was where their paths ended, there was no need to hold her back.

blocks.speaker: Dante
blocks.content: Alright. Take care.

blocks.speaker: Liria
blocks.content: Indifferent bastard…
As Dante truly let her go, Liria felt slighted.
Yet, she didn't look back as she walked away.
Turning around felt like it would make it truly irreversible.

blocks.speaker: Dante
blocks.content: Write if anything fun happens!
Liria blinked at Dante's voice echoing in the distance, then let out a chuckle.
Airhain Dante. 18 years old.
Second in line among the magic aspirants of the Kingdom of Thormia.

blocks.speaker: Liria
blocks.content: Heh. I'll remember that.
With a bright expression, Liria walked towards the horizon as Dante and Amy headed back to the exit.
Eager to leave the now tiresome ruins, they encountered Jokre's group waiting near the ticket barrier.
Their faces were surprisingly defiant.
Unable to completely hide their fear, they seemed unwilling to show weakness.
Vivian approached Amy.

blocks.speaker: Vivian
blocks.content: I'm sorry.
Amy waved her hand.
This wasn't child's play.
Their arrest seemed just in balancing the situation.

blocks.speaker: Vivian
blocks.content: Forgetting it. We both fought with conviction. There's no need to apologize just because you lost.
No. I mean for saving me at the end.
If I had died then, there wouldn't have been a next time.

blocks.speaker: Amy
blocks.content: Well...
Amy awkwardly turned away.
Truthfully, her actions weren't solely for Vivian.
They were necessary to succeed with Okwangbakyojin, and the last shout was something she wanted to say to herself.

blocks.speaker: Amy
blocks.content: I don't know why you said that, but try hard. Make sure you become a magician at the next graduation. You definitely have what it takes to be a protagonist.
Amy was moved.
It was a cruel thought, but words from the lips of a defeated opponent gave her more confidence than any consolation she had heard before.
Vivian limply dropped her shoulders and returned to her friends.
Watching the miserable departure of a loser, Amy spoke up.

blocks.speaker: Amy
blocks.content: Hey.
As Vivian turned around, Amy unbuckled one strap of her bag and rummaged through it as she approached.
Here. Take this.
In Amy's hand was a doll of the Ice Queen, a prize from a mini-game arcade.
Holding it with trembling hands, Vivian stared at the Ice Queen for a long while.
The face of the Ice Queen, who had enveloped her in solitude, smiled innocently like a child.
Sniff. Sob.
Tears streamed down as Vivian hugged the Ice Queen doll close.
Jokre approached and comforted her back.
After a moment, he led her away with a glance at Amy.
Amy watched them leave for a long time.
They would continue to fight in their own lives.
Perhaps, someday, they too could become protagonists.

blocks.speaker: Amy
blocks.content: Yeah. It's just one failure. If this is my life, I'll fight and win as much as I need.
Firmly clenching her fist, Amy turned around.
Her steps towards Dante were now unhindered compared to yesterday.
Γùå Γùå Γùå
Divine Transcendance (1)
In the capital city of Bashuka at the Kaizen School of Swordsmanship.
The current certified level 6 swordsman, Kuan, once called the Sorcerer of Death before injuring his Achilles tendon, was battling the nightmares of an irretrievable past.
Born into a military family, Kuan lost all his family on the battlefield at the age of six.
His uncle took him in only to seize his fortune and send him to a swordsmanship school in another land.
However, he harbored no resentment.
Although he had no one to rely on, his genius allowed him to become a certified level 6 swordsman at just 20 years of age.
The nightmare began with an event ten years ago.
At 21, Kuan was appointed as the second company commander of a search battalion tasked with capturing the founder of the Dark Order, Kointra.
The soldiers did not welcome him.
It was natural for a 21-year-old rookie to join a veteran unit with an average age of 38.
Kuan didn't care either.
To him, a commander, soldiers were merely expendable resources needed to win wars.
Without even time to exchange greetings, Kuan led his troops into the Ghost Forest immediately upon his assignment.
Numerous search parties had entered before but all had lost contact without exception.
The previous company commander had fled back to his homeland citing illness from fear, prompting Kuan's emergency deployment.
But Kuan was confident.

blocks.speaker: Kuan
blocks.content: Even if there really were ghosts in the forest, there was nothing in the world his sword couldn't cut.
I'll capture it. Even if it's a ghost.
The first day passed without incident.
But disaster struck in the early hours as the second company was reorganizing at camp.
Attack! It's an attack!
The sentry's cry was the last sound before disappearing.
Realizing the enemy had breached, Kuan grabbed his sword and rushed out of the barracks.
Other soldiers, each armed, were also alert to the approaching enemy in the dim dawn light.
A strong wind blew. Or rather, it seemed only to resound in everyone's ears.
Kuan reflexively cut through the darkness, but his blade caught nothing.
